CURRENT AFFAIRS.

Belleek pottery is only a drop in the ocean of even

Irish consumption of what is popularly called ‘‘chaney’
 ware. Why does not Ireland make its own cups and
saucers and plates? For the same reason that it does

- not speak its own language. It has not the pluck; it is
an imitative, dependent, and not an original and
initiating country. On the estate of Lord Massereene
in Antrim a find of valuable clay has taken place. Per-
~haps, needless to say, we are not indebted to the Free-
~ man for this piece of Irish information, nor to Mr.
- William Murphy’ s self-styled ““ Nationalist organ,’’ the

Independent ; we discover it in a cutting from the Eng-
lish Western Alorning N ews, which a reader has kindly
sent us.

We read that ‘“the discovery, which concerns an
enormous deposit of china clay at Browndod Mountain,
near Doagh, was really made a considerable time ago,
but owing to the difficulties that were put in the way of
its being worked properly by interested parties, it is
only within the last few weeks a syndicate, consisting of
six gentlemen connected with the Cornish clay, and
with ample capital at their backs, was formed. The
discovery was made in a peculiar way. Some photo-
graphers connected with the Great Western Railway
noticed water which struck them as similar to the Cor-

nish clay water. Several samples were sent to the De-
partment of Agriculture for Ireland for analysis by
experts, and after exhaustive tests the Department re-
plied that the deposit was undoubtedly china clay; but
they were doubtful whether it was of sufficiently
superior quality to make really high-class ware. A
number of local gentlemen, who saw possibilities,
formed themselves into a syndicate, and took the lands
upon which the clay was, but the matter hung fire,
none of them bcmcr experts, and ultimately they aban-
doned the idea.’

However, some Cornish men wanted to know the
reason why. A Cornish firm sent over an expert. A
number of pits have been already sunk and the tests
have given entire satisfaction; the lack of railway
accommodation is a drawback.  An TIrish porcelain
mantfactory might have been set up near this deposit,
but whilst our Mr. Martyns.and our Mr. Sweetmans
are tallking Sinn Fein the wide awake Cornishers come
over here and practise it, from their Cornish point of
view. We presume the clay will be shipped over to
England for use there.

It is announced in the daily Press that Mr.
Buxton, P.M.G., has. written to Mr. Field
stating that he has agreed to the transfer of
part of the Postal Order work from London to

Dublin. We hope Mr. Field will keep pegging away, at
the Savings’ Bank and Money Order work until he
secures the transfer of the Irish accounts from London
to Dublin. But we would above all direct attention to
the necessity of a vigorous agitation to secure for Ire-
land its proper share of expenditure on Post Office
stores. Here is what should be a splendid market for
the Irish manufacturer entirely reserved for the pre-
dominant partner. Why should bicycles be imported
from England for the Irish Post Office service? Cannot
an Irish manufacturer supply a good article at a
moderate cost? We should like to see them and other
manufacturers write to the P.M.G., asking to be
allowed to quote for Irish supplies, delivered in Ire-
land. Tt would be interesting to see what reply would
be vouchsafed by the permanent officials. The LEADER,,
week by week, contains advertisements of such stores as
paints, soaps, tweeds and caps, etc. Why do not the
makers of these keep bombarding the G.P.O. to be
allowed to tender for Irish requirements for delivery in
Ireland? Tt is only by continuous pressure that any
instalment of justice can be secured in these matters,
and surely the £100,000 or so spent by the Irish Post
Office in importing stores from England is worth fight-
ing for. Even in England big contractors fight keenly
for Government contracts.

"THE LEADER.

The Most Rev. Dr. Boylan, the new Bishop of Kil-
more, speaking at a meeting of the Cavan Branch of
the United Irish League, urged his audience to do their
best to make the people encourage Irish industry.
Recently the United Irish League emphasised the in-
dustrial plank in their programme, and we hope, though
we confess we are not over sanguine, that the organisa-
tion will do something, apart from saying much, in the
interests of the industrial revival. Tis Lordship said :—

When he was Provincial of the Redemptorists he had a great
many young people under him and he always gave orders that
they should never use any clothing, shoes, or stockings, ectc., that
were not of Irish manufacture, and they always carried that out.
There was no industry that had madoe so much progress in Ircland
within the last twenty years as the woollen industry. It had
made marvellous progress in the North and the South. They ought
to encourage it, and, in fact, encourage every other home industry
and those who were manufacturing these arficles should take care
to give as good value as thoy got from England. It was unrea-
sonable to expect that the Irish people would make a great sacri-
fice if the manufacturers would not give them as good value as
the English merchants. IHe believed they could casily turn out
as good and as cheap an article because, though their opportunities
were limited, still the wages were considerably lower than in
England.  For that reason they could supply quite as good valuo
as the Englishman, if they only took the pains to do it, and they
aught to be made take the pains to do it.

Tis Lordship also spoke some bracing common sense
about tillage. He said:—

He knew right well that farmers would say that tillage would
not pay, but he knew that was absolutely untruc. Tillage would
pay if they worked as people did in England and Scotland. The
Redemptorist Order had a House in Perth, in the North of Scot-
land, and behind that there was a very high hill. He had been
over there recently, and from the top of that hill he lookod around
and saw thousands and tens of thousands of acres of tillage. There
was very little grass, but he saw oats, wheat, barley, and all sorts
of green crops, and vet neither the climate nor the soil was better
than ours. They had, moreover, to pay much higher wages, and

- still it paid the Scotch farmers, They would not continue tilling if
it did not pay. It will pay us also, said his lordship emphatically,
if we till scientifically, and if we are as industrious as they are.

What really requires tilling is primarily the farmers’
brains; and their energies stand in need of bracing.
Winter dairying, for instance, is a big matter to Ire-
land. Danish butter goes ronund all the year, but Irish
butter is only a six months in the year affair. -When
Irish butter 1s cut of the market for six months it has
to force its way in again, and the Danes, with their
all the year supply, have a natural and very material
advantage, other things being equal, over the Irish
buttermakers. Why don’t the Irish farmers go in for
winter dairying? We are not experts in the butter
trade, and all we can argue is that if the Danes can do
it there must be something wrong in the state of Ire-
land when the Irishman cannot go and do likewise. So
far as we know the British Government, interloper as it
is, does not prevent the Irish dairy farmer, from going
in. for winter dairying. Then, why don’t they go in
for it? 1In reply to a question on the point by the
Chairman of the Commission of Inquiry into the De-
partment, Mr. Anderson, Secretary of the I.A.0.S. (we
are in doubt whether he referred to all Ireland or only
to the south), replied: “ If T was asked for my reason
T would say it was laziness: it does take a good deal
of work. There is another difficulty, that labour is
hard to get in the south, and there is a good deal of
rich land in Limerick, Tipperary, and Cork that is not
suitable for tillage.”

Laziness! We cannot offer a first hand opinion on Mr.
Anderson’s views, but in face of the Danish fact we are
quite willing to believe there is much in it. We do not

JuLy 20, 1907. =

Lolieve the farmers in any part of Ireland are
‘physically lazy as a class, they are mentally lazy, lazy
in-initiative. And whilst not very remote history pro-
vides many excuses for the lack of initiative, the fact
that we cannot live on historical excuses, though com-
munities might, and do, thrive on buttermaking, sug-
gests that we had better forget the history for the time
being, and, facing whatever odds are against us, go in
for winter dairying.

It was the King, the King, everywhere. Leading
articles about the royal visit appeared in the F'reeman
as well as in the Independent, and the ‘‘ Extremists,”
who appear to fade into nothingness except when a
little factious talk or very cheap action is to be done,
did not ‘“hold up’’ the carriage of King Edward.
Probably Edward VIII. of Kildare Street Club went to
his castle in Galway for the occasion, and Mr. John
Sweetman, the voluble patriot, no doubt was glad of
king’s weather—for the sake of the hay.

As we write we have not heard that Mr. William
Murphy has got the wages of ‘‘loyalty.”” He has not
Leen raised to the level of Sir J. A. Power, of Water-
ford, or Sir D. Hegarty, of Cork. He is still Mr.
Murphy—late of Bantry. It is cruel. Clery’s, of
O’Connell, or should we not rather say Sackville, Street,
was decked out like a doll’s house, or a Christmas
bungery, with artificial flowers and bunting in honour
of the King’s visit. And Clery’s is reputed to be a
house much patronised by the ‘‘ Tdolatrous >’ clergy and
laity. According to ome’s individual point of view,
drapery shop was the disgrace or the pride of
Dublin during the King’s visit.

flow is Mr. William Murphy playing his cards?
Clery’s was aglow with decorations, whilst the premises
of his ““ Nationalist organ,” the so-called Independent,
had not even a flag or a rag out. Yet the Independent
newspaper was effusive about the King. Mr. William
Murphy, we see, was at the Humphrey’s Dump
luncheon. Now, after all Clery’s Mr. Murphy is not
“Sir William.”

But where were Mr. Martyn’s party and the other
various bands of “ extremists ”” during the King’s visit ?
The King of England came over here and he was not
met, so far as we are aware or have heard, with one
actively disloyal note; if there were rebels they must
have stayed within the precincts of the Kildare Street
Club or the premises of the ‘“ National Council,” or
kept saying nothing in the streets. Nomne of the few
“rebelly * Civil Servants in the pay of the Government
appear (o have hissed! Ts Dublin ‘“loyal’’ or ‘‘dis-
loyal ’? I she is ‘‘disloyal *’ she liked evidently to
keep the secret to herself during the King’s visit. Had
the King gone to Killarney, Cork, Galway, Waterford,
Limerick, he would probably have been received with
as great, if nol greater, cordiality. Yet some of cur
youngsters get hot over the problem, whether the King,
Lords, and Commons of Ireland, or a Republic should
be regarded as a final settlement—Home Rule, in so far
as it means control over our domestic affairs, as an
ontity within the Empire, is only in the farthing place!
Where is all this ‘“extreme’’” force when the King

comes? Where are Mr. Dixon and Mr. Martyn and
Mr. Sweetman? If we want a republic or
complete separation of some sort, we might

at least pass a resolution, and wave a black

Where, was Alder-
man Kelly? Why did he not pull down the Union
Jacks? The extreme’’ party are ready to make a
““move ’ when the cab is away; when the cat, or the
King, comes Alderman Kelly and his party are too
busy indoors to permit them to pull down ‘ Union
Jacks’’! What was the Rev. Mr. Hannay, of West-
port doing? Is he ‘‘loyal’” also? ~ And M.
McGinley and Mr. Roche? Is a common P.P. their
high water-mark in the way of revolt? Are they
timorous of standing up to the King? Where was
Skeffy, the hyphened democrat? He glared at the
‘“loyal”’ organ at the ‘ Royal’ University. We
have not heard that he even hurled a look of scorn or
even a hyphened grunt at the King! At least we
should have oxpected that, clad in the mantle of
Michael Davitt, he would have mounted the base of
the O’Connell monument and have treated Edward VII.
to a look of democratic scorn! Is it that when the King
comes on the scene the mice of toy ‘‘ rebellion ”’ retire
to their obscurities and their day nurseries %

The fact is, as.we have often said, this is not only
a ““loyal ” but a tame country. We have sufficient
intellectual sincerity to state the case as we see it, but
that sincerity grates on the nerves of the lip-rebels who
do not want to be told the truth. But after all, the
civil servant in the -pay of the Government who de-
nounces all and sundry who are not strong enough on
complete separation, is like the other bogus-rebels, a
subject for laughter and perhaps for pity. The bogus-
rebel is a decided nuisance in this country, as he tends
to bolster up a humbug and lead youngsters into ridi-
culous pesitions.

The King laid the foundation stone of the University
College of North Wales at Bangor, before he came over
6o Ireland. He did not come over here to lay the
foundation stone of a new University College, though
in his speech to Parliament he foreshadowed University
legislation.  In the course of his speech at Bangor,
North Wales, the King said: ‘‘ Competition in every
branch of industry, especially in those branches which
depend largely on science and art, is in these days
severe, and it must be met by increased application and
improved methods. The world is, I believe, better for
such competition, but it hehoves individual nations to
use every possible effort to hold their own in the struggle
for this purpose.  Higher education is an absolute
nccessity.  However brilliant a man’s natural talents
may be, he is greatly hindered by the want of early
training, and as a rule only those who have enjoyed a
good education are capable of acquiring such proficiency
in any branch of study as will enable them to succeed.
The University College of North Wales will offer to its
students exceptional opportunities of instruction.’”
Certainly  higher education is an absolute nccessity,”’
and His Majesty’s “ Liberal ’ Ministers have shelved
the Irish University question!

We were present at the opening of the Summer
Course at the Royal College of Science, Stephen’s Green,
last week. The opening ceremony, if we may call it so,
consisted of an address by Mr. T. P. Gill to the students
assembled in the theatre. The students were, we
believe, all teachers of secondary schools in aclive
service.  Christian Brothers constituted a very large
part of the audience; there were also a large number of
lay toachers and lady teachers, a few priests, and
one nun. . .

The teachers come up to those short summer courses
during their holidays, and Mr. Gill spoke of the educa-

“tional zeal and enthusiasm the teachers displayed in

taking advantage of these courses as ground for great
hope for the educational future of the country. This
is the seventh year of the summer courses, and last year
many students were declared to be fully qualified,
having gone through a five years’ course. The College
of Science is only one of the centres in Dublin where
summer courses are being held, and there are also
courses in various parts of the country. We intend tc
devote a special article shortly to this particular branch
of the work of the Department in our series: ‘‘The
Department in Being,”” so we will not go further inte
the matter just now.

Mr. Martyn’s funny party is getting more attention
from the country just at present than it probably is
thankful for. We see that our contemporary, the Wex-
ford ‘“ Free Press,”’ deals with the party in a leading
article of nearly two columns in length under the cap-
tion ““ The Sinn Fein Humbug.”” The tin-pikers have
abandoned the rhetorical trenches and have fallen back
on tin-feigning.  The tin-feigners are marching on
Enniscorthy next Sunday, and we must say that the
“ Free Press ”’ gave them a rather slashing peppering in
advance. In the course of its leading article our con-
temporary says:—‘‘ The public bodies of County Wex-
ford, so far as they have at present spoken, show no
inclination to mix themselves up with the new-fangled
movement. The Corporation of Wexford, the Urban
Council of Ross, the Enniscorthy Poor Law Board, the
United Irish League Branches of Taghmon and Davids-
town, have declined the invitation to receive an educa-
tion in Nationality from the Dublin Town Councillors
and their friends.””

There is a great deal of twaddle talked about passive:
resistance these days. In view of the newer, or should
we say newest, wisdom, it is a wonder there are any
standing armies and navies in the world! What do
countries want supporting armies and navies when they
could fall back on ‘‘passive resistance’’! The Boers
were fools to have fought the British ; they should have
allowed the British army a walk over, and when the
Union Jack was hoisted at Pretoria, the Boers should
have played their cure-all ‘‘ policy *’—they should have
adopted the policy of passive resistance and humiliated
the British Empire! Why were Japan and Russia
such a pair of fools as to have fought recently? Why
were they not wise, and why did not each adopt the
new cure-all policy of passive resistance!

Passive resistance is all right as an embroidery to po-
tential active resistance. So far as we can analyse it,
passive resistance derives all its power from active resis-
tance in reserve. If there is no reserve of active resis-
tance any fool can snap his fingers at passive resistance.
If you have a loaded revolver in your hand and are pre-
pared to let it off with deadly effect, you may passively
resist the importunity of your tailor’s collector when he
comes for the settlement of the account. If we in Ire-
land had an alternative, if we could fall on London, or
batter Manchester into smithereens, or put bullets into
the heads of everyone of England’s Faithful Garrison,
England would hearken to our passive resistance and
might, nay, would, be glad to give way before it so as
to avoid bringing our active resistance round her ears.
Passive resistance as a policy is like a good diplomatist.
There are expert diplomatists, and muddling dip-
lomatists, but all diplomatists depend in the last analysis
upon what may happen if diplomacy fails. If nothing
can be expected to happen when diplomacy fails, well

Canada of the Snows is not content with the Emigra-
tion advt. for which the ‘“ International ’’ Exhibition
provided a site. We see that the front cover of a recent
issue of the Irish Homestead is covered by a Canadian
Emigration touting advt. Farm lands in Canada are
dangled before the eyes of the readers of the Home-

_ stead. The Homestecad is the official organ of the
I.A.0.S., of which Society our old friend Vigo
Schwartz was once an expert, and the I.A.O.S. gets «
large grant of public money through the Department.
Is it allowable, and in order that such an organ,
ostensibly existing to-promate co-operation, egg-packing,
and such things, should add to its revenue by selling its
front cover to the Canadian Government, who are
anxious to entice Irish farmers to the Lady of the
Snows !

- At forthcoming Oireachtas Exhibition, to be held
from August 2nd to August 10th next, a section will be
. devoted to showing furniture of Irish manufacture
throughout, for the kitchen and rooms of two model
houses—one fitted out in best grade goods and the other
furnished in a more economic fashion suitable for the
working population. The syllabus for Exhibition can
be had on application to Hon. Secretary, Industrial
Committee, Gaelic League, Dublin. Particulars of Art
Section can be had from Hon. Secretary, Art Com-
mittee. Exhibition will open on 2nd August.

last week’s article on ‘‘ The Department, Mr. Micks,
and the Foreign Gentlemen > :— The majority Re-
- Port, we venture to think, will not be read by twenty
people other than those who subscribed their names to
ib.  Mr. Micks’ minority Report runs to 165 folio
Pages, quite a month’s reading for most men who have
their own business to look after.’” This is how it
appeared in last week’s issue: — The majority Report,
we venture to think, will not be read by twenty people
other than those who subscribed 165 large pages, quite
a month’s reading for most men who have their own
business to look after.” We are quite cool and recon-
ciled to the inevitable now! And we hope that the
recording angel will shed a tear at our plight and blot
out the extra virile language that the occasion
prompted !

But the printers did more than that last week. In
the announcement of the forthcoming Munster Feis
they printed: ““ Don't miss €ogan Ruav O NéilL the
greatest and most elaborate Irish DANCE yet produced
on the Irish Ireland stage.” We have put the word
‘““dance’’ in capitals. One of the authorities of the

' Feis very generously wrote us saying that he did not
expect us to murder anybody as punishment for this
blunder. ‘I should be very sorry to do so,”” he writes,
““But if you would kindly draw attention to the Drama
in next week’s LEaDER it would satisfy everybody.
€03 aii Ruad O NéilLis, as you are aware, an Irish Drama

written by (two of) the members of the Columbian League
in Maynooth, and, with the exception of its production
at Maynooth, it is being produced for the first time at
the forthcoming Munster Feis.”’

One of those who collaborated in the production of
the play is at present on his holidays, and he is work-
ing hard for the play in Cork. He has behind him the
Dramatic Corps of the North Parish Branch, and so the
production of the drama “ €ogan Rusd O héiL’”
may reasonably be looked forward to with great ex-
pectations. It will be produced twice during the Feis.

We are requested to state that there are two or there
schools in the district of Iorrus where the language is
being shamefully neglected. In one of these, viz.,
Scoil Dubhthuama, in the parish of Beannchair, all the
pupils can speak it; the assistant teacher and the
monitor have a very good knowledge of it, both oral and

ANTI-PARLIAMEN FARIANISM IS NOT=
NATIONALITY.

NE of the works which we set ourselves to do was
O to dispose of the rather vague, but prevalent
and nation-killing fallacy that politics and nationality
were convertible terms. That fallacy is exploded now,
but another, and a somewhat similar one, appears to be
forming—that is, that nationality is a policy, and that
that policy is anti-Parliamentarianism! The Parlia-
mentarians had more or less roped in nationality, and it
would appear tkat the anti-Parliamentarians are now
endeavouring to do a like ill service—to rope nationality
inside what they call a ‘‘ policy.”” The anti-Parliamen-
tarians want to make out that nationality is a ““ policy !"*
If you are against the anti-Parliamentarian ticket you
are anathema! Sinn Fein, as we have often pointed
out, is not a policy ; it is merely a rallying cry, and it
is futile, to go no further, for the anti-Parliamentarians
to grab it. The ‘‘loyal’’ thief who stole the trinkets from
Dublin Castle will not be a Knight of St. Patrick when
he puts them on. Sinn Fein merely means self-reliance
or self-help, and it is a cry, and a good one, and nothing
more.

Parliamentarianism is not nationality ; but neitker is
anti-Parliamentarianism. The tiller of the soil is entitled
to cry /Sinn Fein, whatever his theories may be about
Republics or other things.  Mr. Sweetman, wko, we
understand, does not till his land to any considerable
oxtent, and who does not run an industry, is free to
ovate Sinn I'cin, though whether with as good a title
as the humble delver we leave it to our readers to decide.
The attempt to make anti-Parliamentarianism synony-
mous with nationality must fail, but the attempt is per-
nicious and stupid where it is not dishonest. We have
no great faith in the efficiency of the present Parlia-
mentary Party, but Parliamentary action is at least as
necessary to effective political activity in Ireland as are
clerks and office boys to a trading firm; and that cer-
tainly is not placing the Parliamentary department of
the Irish firm very kigh. But anti-Parliamentarianism

't Dublin has an atmosphere of its own as surely
as London has not, is probably due to the distinctive
 architecture of its older quarters. The dignity of these
eighteenth century streets giows on one more and more.
In the dm.‘_uesf;xc architecture of medizval Spain a great
- restraint is _Observable as far as the exterior is con-
cerned. Quite plain walls, uncorniced, almost without
ornament, except 2 ropelike moulding, representing the
cord of St. Francis, let fall over doorways and window

openings. This habit of building the Spaniards caught
up from the Moors, who practised it through religious
motives.  One knows not where Dublin learned its

restraint, but wayfaring through these streets you find
yowrself over and over again reflecting on what manner
of people built them. Cultured brows, they suggest,
eminent respectability, and a social life free from pr
tence. It is difficult to think of them in connection
with duelling bucks and Hellfire revellings, difficult to
think of them in connection with that period of Irish
listory at all. Of course, the life of Dublin at that
time, and the life of Ireland, had little or nothing in
common.

But Dublin has gone away from its own self. About
Grafton Street, what have you but a bit of provincial
London—the same unrest, the same jumbling togethor
.of stone, brick, and bits of terra-cotta—catalogue archi-
tecture, without calmness, without dignity. Stand
waiting for a friend at any street-corner hereabout—
you have little freshness to see, little novelty, in spite of
the absence of any type. There is none of that con-
sidered chastened beauty which comes when the bizarre
has been put by as well as the commonplace; there is
nothing that you may not see on a smaller scale in any
provincial town where the metropolitan banks have
built. themselves branch offices. But, for that matter,
the fine old decency which built the spacious residential
squares seems to be lost to the modern world, let alone
the capital of an impoverished country. It is surely
well though to know a city where so much of it holds its
own.

The building in the newer suburbs is surely very

cheap.  No character here—nobody will trouble his
head in future time about the builder. The
houses seem to have been designed with the

aid of a box of toy building bricks. The pity of it,
considering that in most cases the architect was, we may
be sure, given a fairly free hand. No twelve-feet-square

- pane of plate glass to be built round, as happens in the
case of the modern shop, no adjoining houses to be
reckoned with. Still the use of cement in these suburbs
is rare—a thing to be thankful for.

When one has lived in Dublin for some time he
Liecomes.a Dubliner at least in one respect; whole dis-
tricts, by far the larger part of the city, remain un-
visited from one end of the year to the other. I have
heard of a certain citizen who regarded all parts north
‘of the Rotunda, south of Stephen’s Green, as waste
places; he boasted of keeping near the heart of things.
Certainly large areas seem to have long ago parted com-
pany with the heart of things. Dublin is like a gar-
ment that wants trimming.  Yet these unvisited places
are the most interesting ; they are also the most histori-
cal; they are the places one may be sure Victor Hugo
would have selected to form, terrorise, overwhelm, kill
the minds of his characters in a romance of the city:
they are Dublin, most pregnantly. :

'On the whole our mother city is a fine old place, too
big and noble, indeed, out.of proportion with our
country. Would that Dublin remembered this. I should
like that it had more nerve, politically and socially.
The centre of politics shifting, as it is, from the fields
to the streets. Dublin must see respect to itself. Just
a little more daring; just a little less respect for Horse
Shows, etc., and other annual expedients to keep the
pot boiling.

AY I claim the hospitality of the columns of the

Leaper to make some observations in reply to

two writers who have treated the above subject in the

two preceding issues? The first signs Fimself *“ Sacerdos,
D.D.,”” and the second ‘‘ Sagart.” e

As regards ‘“ Sacerdos, D.D.,”” whom for brevity sake
I will call *‘Sac., D.D.,;”’
He is a priest, a school manager, is not young nor igno-
rant of Canon Law ; has discussed the subject with fore-
most Canonists at home and abroad, and is a Doctor
of Divinity. Some persons may be disposed to question
the good taste of parading all these credentials ; but ““ de
gustibus non est disputandum.”  Much would natu-
rally be expected from a writer with such an array of
qualifications. But I venture to say that his readers were
sadly disappointed. He has descended into the arena.
somewlat like Goliah of old; but he is vulnerable at
many points.

He says that much light was not thrown in previous
writings on above subject because of an undue indul-
gence in personalities. ~ Why, then, occupy so much
space with his own personality and personal qualifica-
tions? e says that unnecessarily strong language was
used during the controversy. From this one would
espect the manners and language of a Chesterfield from
himself. Glancing through his article one finds the fol-
lowing gems:—‘‘ To advance exaggerated, unwarrant-
able or unjust claims is bad policy . . . .it is suicidal.”
This, as far as I can see, can refer only to the Bishops’
declaration, which I quoted more than once, regarding
the school management. Again—‘‘ The present conflict
between theory and fact is a nuisance.”” And further
on, alluding to the use of the word ‘anti-clerical,’”
which cropped up during tlke discussion, he says—‘‘ Tt
is a crying scandal, and monster injustice.”” And still
further on it ‘‘is unchristian and unwarrantable, an
abominable and barbarous tyranny.”* There is here, T
submit, a rather remarkable contrast between preach-
ing and practice on the part of ‘“Sac., D.D.”

But, passing over this exhibition of strong language,
we may expect a good deal of fresh information on the
subject under discussion from one so full of credentials.
What do we find? He lays down three propositions of
Canon Law, known to every tyro in theology, and to
every well-educated layman, and on whick I touched in
the course of one of my articles in reply to Inquirer.
So far, so good. One would naturally expect from the
wiiter that he would at once devote himself to the
application of his principles to the question of ‘‘ primary
school management in Ireland.” Yet, strange to say,
having laid down his principles, he shirks their appli-
cation, for he says—‘‘I do not propose to discuss in
detail the respective rights of the Church and secular
authority in regard to the control of education, etc.’”
That is to say, the very thing everybody naturally ex-
pected him to discuss he does not discuss at all. - Why,
then, did he enter into the controversy?
Why, then, criticise the barren-
ness of previous discussions? But wkilst he declines to
apply his principles to the concrete case of school
management in Ireland, he begins to build castles in
the air, and applies his principles to a state of affairs
non-existent in Ireland, nor likely ever to exist as long
as we are under foreign rnle. He theorises about the

he gives us his credentials. ~

Why parade .

:gbqte of. affairs that should exist in countries (few,
indeed, if any, at present) in which the Church and
State work hand-in-hand, each recognising in theory
and fact the rights of the other. Surely something
more practical and conducive to the public enlighten-
ment than this ought to have been given by * Sac.,
D.D.”” But if his contribution as a canonist has hecn
unsatisfactory, it is rather astonishing as a school
anager. For he tells us that—“In theory our system
‘of primary education is at present undenominational,
as it Las been from the beginning. Religious instruc-
tion forms no official part of it.’’ I think there is a
contradiction between these two sentences. If religious
instruction forms no official part of it, then it is purely
secular. But if it is undenominational, then it is not
purely secular, because at least undenominational reli-
gious instruction is an official part of it. TFow reconcile
these two statements? It is not only contradictory,
but, to my mind, inaccurate, to style it either undenomi-
national or purely secular. It is in theory what, from
the beginning, it professed to be-—a. system of combined
literary and separate religious instruction.  This
latter element had in it the seed of what now exists—
namely, that whatever it be in name it has developed
inte a practically denominational system, because it
afforded the managers of the schools the opportunity of
giving separate denominational religious instruction to
the children of their own persuasion. It does not, to
be sure, come up to the perfect Catholic ideal of a deno-
winational system; but, in the words of the United

Catholic Hierarchy, ‘“ it has been gradually transformed

iuto a form that is in harmony with the actual condi-

tion of the country.”

Again ““ Sac., D.D.,” tells us that the civil authority
now ‘ practically builds the school.””  From this I
conclude that he has not been building schools lately ;
if he had been, he would not have made such an erro-
neous statement. It is also evident that he is not in
touch with his fellow-managers, whko have been clamour-
ing, and in vain, for an increased proportion of build-
ing grants on account of the changed circumstances of
the times. I have been for years, and still am, the
manager of several schools, and am not aware of any
change in “he rules and practice of the National Board
which would warrant ‘“ Sac., D.D.,”” in making such a
statement. Any change that has taken place has been
for tke worse, by completely withholding building grants
for a number of years.

As “Sac., D.D.,” is so disappointing as a canonist,
and uninformed as a manager of schools, his views and
theories regarding the managerial question will, T think,
‘recommend themselves to very few who have been
studying this question. His attempt to explain and
justify the pronouncement of the Bishops is rather un-
called for, if not grotesque, in one who recommends a
Jittle later a course of action diametrically opposed to
thz direction of the Bishops.

“Tlis is, doubtless,” he says, ‘‘ what the Trish
Bishops have in their minds when they say, etc.”” Does
not this imply that the Trish Bishops do not know how
to express themselves, and that it required the gloss of
““ Sac., D.D.,”” to make their minds clear? Why, the
language of the Biskops is the clearest and simplest that
can be imagined. Let me quote it once again:—

As authoritative statements made recently in Parliament indicate

not sufficiently understood, we deem it our duty to make the fol-
lowing statement: —

That we feel that any limitation or restriction of the control, which
is 10w exercised by managers over the schools of the National
system ‘of education would be so injurious to the religious intorests

“of our peaple, as to make it imperative on us to resist the intro-

duction of such a measure, and, in case it were adopted, to recon-
sider our wholo position in relation to these schools.

I speak for nobody beyond myself, and I seldom take
my views from anybody else; but the Hierarchy, it
secms to me, have to concern themselves with the prac-
tical rather tkan the speculative side of the education
question.  They know, under the very hand of the
plotters themselves, the insidious objects in which the
so-called National system had its origin. They know
further that at every stage of its existence attempts
(waxing fainter by degrees as they began to spend them-
selves in ill-success) were made to proselytise and Angli-
cise the pupils. ~And for these good and sufficient
reasons, which have been within their cognisance from
the first, they gave the Government the choice of dele-
gating tlis managerial power to the clergy, or else of
having the schools left scholarless, so far as Catholic
children were concerned.  As faithful shepherds the
Bishops could not otkerwise save the lambs committed
to their care by God. The prime consideration with
tlem then, as now, was the safeguarding of faith and
morals; and the quarter from which the educational
fund came could only be a very secondary element in
a matter of such grave importance. As I said at an
carlier stage of the controversy, although the danger of
proselytism may be past, a greater threatens. Secu-
larism it is that now looms largely on tkLe horizon the
world over, and find most favour with the ruling majo-
rity of those forty millions of sectaries and atheists by
whom, as mentioned in my last article, we arc domi-
nated. The very latest system of education that these,
our masters, have favoured us with—the technical—
is quite a godless one. And yet, charged, as it osten-
sibly is, with the instruction of a mere fraction of tkLe
youth of the country, the Government endowed it with
a predominating power, in the proportion of three to
two, in the Co-ordination Committee, which had to deal
with all the systems below the University. Evidently
it was the model on which it was intended to fashion tke
general system of local control in the matter of educa-
tion. Further, it must be borne in mind that it is under
this syvstem of local control, dominated by central
bureaucracies, as ih the cas> of the Irish Technical Com-
mittees—which have no real power or liberty—that reli-
gion has been driven from tke schools in other countries.
And we may rest assured that the ultimate object which -
the English Radicals would have in view in extending
local control to Ireland would be to get the priests out-
side the schools, and, as a corollary, the Catholic reli-
gion also. We can be under no misapprehension as to
the views of the Massies, the MacNamaras, and the
M‘Kennas and their followers on tke point. Observe
their latest move calculated to swamp the English Catho- .
lic Training Colleges with Protestants and atheists, and
all, of course, as a corollary of local control.

This managerial controversy, specially manufactured
for the occasion, was sprung on the public during the
period of incubation of the late Council Bill. It was
in its early stages machined, no doubt, by persons
acquainted with—perhaps the very authors of—the
educational provisions embodied in that Bill—in esse or
in posse. And these plotters found abettors—and dupes
in good faith, I grant, also—who settled themselves
down to smooth the way for the coming bantling. And
the Bishops—men of wide worldly experience, high in-
telligence, and deep knowledge of educational move-
ments, of the past and of tlhe present, at home and
abroad—detected the impending danger, and, like
watchmen on the tower, gave warning. And the men
of our faith, lay or cleric, sticklers for Canon Law
though they may be, who will disregard or attempt to
belittle such warning, are not true to the spirit or tradi-
tions of the Church. And although their action may
not, technically, be anti-Catkolic (I have never myself
applied this eopithet to them), still, in my humble

BS0ME EXHIBITS AT THE EXHIBITION.

HE modern steamship is an appropriate symbol of
industrial progress. The exhibits of Harland and

Wolff at the Exhibition at Herbert Park set one hope-
fully thinking of the potentialities of Irish development
in other lines of industry. By the way, those exhibits re-
called a comment I heard made on the new manufacture
of cutlery in Dublin to the effect tlat all its materials
had to be imported, which curious view of Irish manu-
facture I rebutted by remarking that such a rigid stan-
dard would put even Harland and Wolff’s outside the
pale of an Irish manufacturing industry. One of the
models shown at the stand of the Dublin Port and
Docks Board set me wondering if dredgers are made at
the Queen’s Island, as the card on the model referred to
gave the information that this ‘‘Suction Hopper "
Dredger ”’ was constructed for the Board as far away
from Ireland as Holland. Among other exhibits here,
however, I noticed a ‘‘ Diving Bell and Plant,”” made
at Drogheda in the year 67, and a ‘‘ Sheers Float ” of
similar date made by Harland and Wolff at Belfast. The
Board is evidently not a Dark Brother in advertising its
present- efficiency, and any members of it passing by .
this stand might be excused a little swelling of pride at
the skilful way in which modern facilities are empha-
sised by the display of contemporary protests relating to
former disadvantages.

There is an interesting display.of brewery and other
machinery at the stand of Messrs. W. Spence, including
an engine built for Guinness and Co. I was glad to
read the announcement here that “all the exhibits'
on this stand were made in my own works at Cork St.,
Dublin.”

If all the corks drawn in Ireland were also cut here,
cork-cutting would probably be a very important Irish
industry. Messrs. Edmunds make an effective display
in this line, their exhibits including a sample of the
raw material of corks in the shape of a small cork tree.
Toilet soap, in the making, is a feature at the stand of
Messrs. J. B. Barrington and Sons, who give practical
illustrations daily of the processes by which their popu-
lar product is manufactured. Anotker most interesting
working exhibit is that of Williams and Woods, Ltd.,
Dublin, the well-known manufacturers of sweets, jams,
etc. IHere several workers are busily engaged turning
out all kinds of sweetmeats, and the appetising odour
pervading this stand is a magnet to visitors, particularly
of the younger sort.

AN ORANGEMAN ON ORANGE HUMBUG. 
T is not because Mr. Lindsay Crawford was at one 
time editor of that virulent, if obscure, publication, 
the " Irish Protestant," that he is not possibly suhject 
to liberalising, and, to some extent, Irish natio1ml in-
Huences. 
The fact that he and his " Independent " 
Orangemeu are not loved by the real out-and-out Orange 
deadheads and drum-thumpers is in his favour from the 
Irish point of view. Personally, we know nothing of 
Mr. Crawford, but we are willing to believe that he is 
honest and sincere. 
He fought and fights his corner 
hard; and as we are people who put up a stiff fight for 
our own views without hemming or hawing about it, we 
confess Lo a certain admiration for a man like Mr. Craw-
ford. It is plain, anyway, that his eyes have begun to 
open. 
\Ve commenced by fighting the humbug and cant 
and nonsense on the green Nationalist side, and we 
therefore have a sympathy and admiration for Mr. 
Crawford in his work of tackling the old Orange dead-
heads, and standing up to the cant, humbug and rcdmeis 
of the traditional Orange Lodge. 
Mr. Crawford, speaking at an "Independent" 
Orange demonstration at Maghermorue on the day after 
the " twelfth," in tl;e course of his speech as reported in 
the " Belfast News-Letter," said :-" That afternoon he 
saw around him tenant-farmers and labourers, and he 
would ask them what bad Orangeism, as represented by 
Colonel Wallace and Colonel M'Calmont, done foi: them 1 
It had not only done nothing, but had done much to 
prevent legislation. 
The .land question could not stand 
where it was. 
In face of the calamitous year t1-at 
Lhreatened the farmers the Government would be com-
pelled in the interest of Irish ratepayers on· whose 
shoulders the financial responsibility for land purchase 
rested, and in the interest of the farmers, who were 
threatened with famine and dire disaster as a result of 
t.lie bad weather, to protect buyers against th-e exorbi-
tant demands of the landlords, and to relieve the rate-
pay~rs from the losses on flotation of land stock. 
What 
had the old Orange Institution done to root the farmer 
in the soil 1 What had it done to root. the labourer in 
the country 1 
The Orange representatives in Parlia-
ment had done their best to prevent the labourers from 
obtaining cottages and land, without which ·labour would 
be attracted to other countries to the great injury of- the 
farmers. 
Ulster Orange representatives in Parliament 
had done their best to prevent the labourers from 
obtaining justice in this matter. He had only to men-
tion the action of the Hillsborough District Council, and 
to refer to Lord Ashtown's admission before an inquiry 
by a Local Government Board Inspector, when he said 
he preferred sycamore trees on his estate rather thau 
Roman Catl::olic labourers." 
This is bold and really independent speaking, and we 
applaud Mr. Crawford for his pluck, even though, we 
suppose, he bas not yet attained such e. clearness of 
vision as to see us in any light but that . of a terror to 
the peace and happiness of the Commonwealth. 
By 
the ,vay, Lord Ashtown is the compiler of the Carrion 
Crow magazine called " Grievances from Ireland. " 
Again, iu the course of his speech, Mr. Crawford 
said:-" Only the other \veek at Ballycarry Colonel 
M'Calmont attributed all the woes in Ireland to the 
Roman Catholic Church and priests, and yet he bought 
Roman Catholic votes in East Antrim, and was able 
to command them on the day of the poll by liberal 
subscriptions to that Church, which at Orange meet-
ings he pretended to regard as the seven plagues ·of 
Egypt. 
(Cheers). 
It might be news to them at 
Magheramorne that Colonel M'Calmont was. very near 
joining the Independents just at the time when he 
(Mr. Crawford) was expelled. 
Colonel _J\l~'Calmont was 
tried in the Grand Lodge for subscnbmg to Lame 
Roman Catholic. Church, but he saved his neck by 
apologising. 
(Laughter). 
~'hey could un<lerstan~, 
therefore, what the Protestantism and professed opposi-
tion to the unic,n of Orange and Green meant with men 
who in turn fooled both Protestants and Roman 
Catholics."

: OF the cant and humbug with reference to the Uni-
Yersxby question in which the bigots of the old order
indulge, Mr. Crawford remarked :—‘ Where were they

~ to find in any of the Protestant Churches an honest

§tand for non-sectarian education? Were not the facts
in Treland proof that the Protestant Churches were as
kesnly desirous of maintaining denominationalism as
the Roman Catholic hierarchy? Were not Mr. Balfour
and his party pledged to the maintenance of sectarian
education?  Had not Trinity College, through her
parliamentary representative, Sir Edward Carson, pro-
posed to concede the full demand of the Roman Catholic
bishops if only Trinity was left alone?’ Dealing with
the higher education question, the speaker said the
Independents in their manifesto and in their resolutions
on the 12th had supported what was known as Bryce’s
scheme—and which had been adopted also by the
General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church. The
truth of the matter was that all parties had been driven
to admit the fact that, whether under a nationalised
Dublin University or under a remodelled Royal Uni-
versity, it was of the essence of the scheme that a college
as Roman Catholic as Trinity was Protestant should
be provided for Roman Catholics. It was dishonest,
therefore, for the leaders of the old Order to pretend
that they were opposed to a settlement of this question
on lines which, while not sectarian, would at the same
time generously concede to the Roman Catholic laity
that distinctive protection for their faith which Trinity
guaranteed to Protestants. Mr. Bryce’s scheme, out-
lined and foreshadowed in their manifesto two years
ago, was pre-eminently the best scheme before the
country, and, as he had pointed out, was backed by the
General Assembly.”’

We don’t believe in the humbug of uniting orange
and green; but we are willing to believe that a great
many of the dupes of the orange dead-heads may have
their eyes opened and see that they have everything to
gain and nothing to lose by throwing in their lot with
the Irish nation, of which by birth, at least, they are
members. We, therefore, would not like to do any-
thing to hinder the development Irishwards of Mr.
Lindsay Crawford and his friends. We freely confess
that we regard his spirited attack on the old Order as
an earnest of his sincerity, even though we are not in
the least likely to forget that he, too, is an Orangeman.
